story:
In the misty Perak highlands, three youths—Afiq, always hungry for the new; Muthu, loyal and steady; and Lewellyn, boldest of the lot—set out to debunk a new version of the legend of the Stone Ogre, Sang Kelembai. The forest closed in, damp and hushed. Without warning, a rocky hand lunged from the earth. They dove, with seconds to spare, as the ground thundered and a towering Mecha-Ogre tore free of the trees, roaring like a mountain given a voice.

Pew!

A shimmering beam traced their outlines. Their bodies locked mid-scramble—faces calm, hearts rattling. Thoughts fizzled to sparks. A shaft in the robot’s armored chest irised open. Metal claws hoisted them still rigid and pitched them into a cell so tight their frozen bodies were wedged upright like tools in a box. A moment later the paralysis ebbed.

“What use are we to this behemoth?” Afiq muttered.

The intercom crackled: “You are going to be INTERROGATED!”

A floating platform ferried them through iron corridors into a magnificent golden chamber. High on a shining dais stood—

“Daleks! And—Zygons?” Lewellyn blurted.

“You know them?” Muthu asked.

“My family worked at Torchwood,” Lewellyn whispered.

“Be silent!” screeched a Reconnaissance Dalek. “State your purpose in these mountains!”

“We were hiking—” Afiq began.

“The Zygons do not believe you,” declared their leader, Salest. “You have been spying on us. You threaten our plans.”

“Shut it, you squid-faced villain!” Lewellyn snapped. “That’s not true!”

“The Daleks concur: you are enemies,” rasped the Dalek. “The verdict shall be—”

“They will be killed!” barked the Zygons.

“Negative!” the Dalek countered. “They will be converted. We require more Daleks. Malaysia is strategic. From Malaysia, Asia will be subjugated. From Asia, the world. The Daleks will be supreme!”

“I disagree,” Salest snarled. “Daleks are selfish. This alliance is a mistake!”

The chamber erupted—shouts, screeches, clicking claws, blazing lamps. The three friends edged backward, then ran. A Dalek swiveled, spotted them, and gave chase, its weapon thrumming with lethal charge. They sprinted toward a narrow maintenance slit cut through the Mecha-Ogre’s stone-clad plating. The Dalek aimed. Its gun hummed higher.

“Vision impaired!” it shrieked.

Lewellyn had slapped his hat over its eyestalk. In that breath of chaos, they jumped. Wind and mist swallowed them as they tumbled onto a steep, mossy ledge outside, skidding down through fern and scree. Behind them, inside the Robo-Beast, the blinded Dalek careened sideways and smashed a weathered lever—a failsafe, ancient and crude.

Pooom!

A shudder ran through the entire golem. The golden lair seized and went silent. The Mecha-Ogre hardened, layer by layer, metal veins turning to stone, gears to gravel. The monstrous body froze where it stood, transformed from roaring titan to silent cliff. Over seasons, then years, rain and roots and time did their quiet work. The colossal figure cracked, eroded, and slumped into scattered boulders and weathered ridges, leaving a jagged memory in the hills—and entombing the quarrelsome invaders for good.

People who wandered the Perak highlands pointed to the broken stones and told the old legend: Sang Kelembai, the Stone Ogre, forever sleeping. And somewhere below, three friends remembered the day a myth was metal, the day a hat beat a death ray, and the day monsters arguing with each other accidentally saved a country none of them was ready to lose.
